title: Dog Torn Nail: First Aid for an Exposed Quick
meta_desc: Learn how to treat a dog's ripped nail and exposed quick. Understand the urgency, first aid steps, and why professional triage is necessary.
content_html:
What is an Exposed Quick?

A dog's nail is composed of a hard outer shell made of keratin and a sensitive inner core known as the quick. The quick contains a rich supply of blood vessels and nerves. When a nail is torn, split, or ripped off entirely, this vascular tissue is exposed. Because the quick is directly attached to the bone of the toe, an injury here is significantly more painful than a simple skin scrape and often results in profuse bleeding.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for a ripped nail is Medium. While it is rarely a life-threatening situation, it is a painful injury that requires prompt attention. You should aim to see a veterinarian within 24 hours. Professional care is necessary to remove any dangling nail fragments safely, manage pain with appropriate pet-safe medications, and prevent a secondary infection from reaching the bone.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

If you are consulting a vet via tele-triage, a clear photo of the injury is incredibly helpful. High-quality images allow a professional to determine if the nail plate is still partially attached, evaluate the level of inflammation, and check for signs of existing infection. This information helps your veterinary team decide if your dog needs to be seen immediately or if the injury can be managed at home until a scheduled appointment.

faqs.question: How can I stop the bleeding at home?
faqs.answer: Apply styptic powder, cornstarch, or flour to the tip of the nail and apply firm, steady pressure with a clean cloth for 5 to 10 minutes.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human pain medication?
faqs.answer: No, never give your dog human medications like Ibuprofen or Tylenol, as these can be toxic. Your vet will provide safe, canine-specific pain relief.

faqs.question: What should I do if the nail is still hanging on?
faqs.answer: Do not attempt to pull it off yourself, as this can cause further damage and extreme pain. Keep the foot bandaged and let a veterinarian remove it using proper tools.
